slides.title: Default Parameters 🎛️
slides.content:
# Optional Parameters

You can give parameters **default values**, making them optional:

```
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    print(f"{greeting}, {name}!")

greet("Alice")           # Hello, Alice!
greet("Bob", "Hey")      # Hey, Bob!
```

If the caller doesn't provide a value, the **default kicks in**. If they do, it **overrides** the default.

⚠️ **Rule:** Default parameters must come **after** non-default parameters!

slides.title: Defaults in Action 🎬
slides.codeSnippet:
def create_profile(name, age, role="Student"):
    print(f"Name: {name}")
    print(f"Age: {age}")
    print(f"Role: {role}")
    print()

create_profile("Alice", 20)              # Uses default
create_profile("Bob", 35, "Teacher")     # Overrides

quizData.question: Where must default parameters be placed?
quizData.explanation: Default parameters must come after required (non-default) parameters.

slides.title: Keyword Arguments 🏷️
slides.content:
## Named Arguments

**Keyword arguments** let you specify arguments **by name**, so order doesn't matter:

```
def order(item, quantity, size):
    print(f"{quantity}x {size} {item}")

# Positional (order matters)
order("Coffee", 2, "Large")

# Keyword (order doesn't matter!)
order(size="Small", item="Tea", quantity=1)
```

Keyword args make your code more **readable** and less error-prone, especially with many parameters!

quizData.question: Can you change the order of arguments when using keyword arguments?

options.text: Yes, keyword args can be in any order

quizData.explanation: Keyword arguments are matched by name, not position, so order doesn't matter.
